skill.name: codebase-cleanup-deps-audit
skill.description: Audit project dependencies for vulnerabilities, license risks, outdated packages, supply chain concerns, and remediation options.
skill.summary: Review dependency security, licensing, update priority, and remediation plans across common package ecosystems.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but the reviewed files contain documentation and code examples rather than executable skill code. Most backtick and weak-crypto alerts are false positives from Markdown fences or security terminology. The remaining risk is medium because the skill guides agents toward network-backed package checks and remediation commands that can alter dependency files.

medium_findings.title: Remediation Examples Can Modify Project Dependencies
medium_findings.description: The playbook includes shell examples that run package manager fixes, update packages, run tests, and revert files. This is legitimate for a dependency audit skill, but it can change manifests and lockfiles if an agent runs it without review. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for operational risk, not malicious intent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and directly tied to dependency remediation. The risk is bounded because they are examples in documentation, not automatically executed code.

medium_findings.title: Network Queries Send Package Data to External Services
medium_findings.description: The playbook recommends vulnerability and bundle-size checks against public package services. Package names and versions may be sent outside the local environment, which users should approve for private projects. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for network exposure risk, with legitimate purpose.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network endpoints and request examples are explicit. No credential exfiltration or malicious domain evidence was found.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick and Weak Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The listed Ruby backtick and weak cryptographic algorithm detections occur in Markdown code fences, prose, or security-related terms. I found no Ruby shell execution, no weak hash implementation, and no instruction to hide malicious behavior. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for those static patterns.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are Markdown skill instructions or fenced examples. No executable Ruby backtick call or weak cryptographic operation is present in the reviewed content.

low_findings.title: Generated Issue Template May Reveal Vulnerability Counts
low_findings.description: The GitHub Actions example can create an issue when critical dependency vulnerabilities are found. The body is brief, but public issue creation can disclose security status if used in a public repository. Verdict: NEEDS_REVIEW for deployment context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow example clearly creates an issue, but repository visibility and actual deployment choices determine exposure.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Manager Commands With Write Effects
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include forced audit fixes, package updates, dependency installation checks, and lockfile rollback. Agents should request approval before running these commands on user repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and can modify local files. They are documented remediation steps rather than hidden execution paths.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Package Metadata Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples call package registries and Bundlephobia APIs. This can expose package inventory metadata from private projects.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoints and request fields are explicit. I found no evidence of secrets being sent.

content.value_statement: Dependency risk is hard to prioritize across package managers, licenses, and transitive packages. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through vulnerability review, compliance checks, and remediation planning.
actual_capabilities: Inventories dependency manifests for npm, Python, Ruby, Java, Go, Rust, PHP, and .NET projects.
Guides vulnerability checks against package advisory sources and prioritizes issues by severity and exposure.
Reviews dependency licenses for compatibility and flags restrictive or unknown licenses.
Prioritizes outdated packages using security impact, release age, version distance, and update effort.
Provides report templates for executive summaries, remediation plans, pull requests, and monitoring workflows.
limitations: It depends on available manifests, lockfiles, and advisory data quality.
It cannot guarantee that every transitive vulnerability is known or exploitable.
License guidance is not legal advice and may need counsel review.
Automated update commands can break builds and should be tested before merging.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Security Upgrade Sprint
use_cases.description: Identify vulnerable packages, group fixes by urgency, and produce a testable remediation plan.

use_cases.title: Review Open Source License Exposure
use_cases.description: Check dependency licenses for incompatible, unknown, or restrictive terms before a release.

use_cases.title: Clean Up Dependency Maintenance Debt
use_cases.description: Find stale packages, high-impact bundle dependencies, and risky supply chain signals.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Dependency Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this repository dependencies. Summarize manifests found, vulnerable packages, license concerns, and the safest first fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Prioritize Security Updates
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these dependency audit results. Rank fixes by severity, exploitability, runtime exposure, and likely upgrade risk.

prompt_templates.title: Assess License Compliance
prompt_templates.prompt: Check dependency licenses against our project license. Flag incompatible, unknown, and restrictive licenses with recommended next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Remediation Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced CI and pull request planning.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a staged dependency remediation plan with test gates, pull request grouping, rollback points, and continuous monitoring.

output_examples.input: Audit a Node.js service before release.
output_examples.output: A release-focused report with dependency counts, critical vulnerabilities, license blockers, upgrade order, and validation tasks.

output_examples.input: Review Python requirements for maintenance risk.
output_examples.output: A prioritized package list showing stale versions, security-sensitive upgrades, compatibility notes, and manual review items.

output_examples.input: Prepare a security dependency pull request.
output_examples.output: A pull request plan with grouped updates, expected risk, test coverage, rollback steps, and reviewer checklist.

best_practices: Run scans from a trusted network and confirm before sending private package names to external services.
Group risky upgrades into small pull requests with clear tests and rollback steps.
Verify license findings with source package metadata and legal review when exposure is material.
anti_patterns: Running forced audit fixes directly on production branches.
Treating advisory severity as the only priority signal.
Publishing detailed vulnerability findings in public issues without approval.

faq.question: Does this skill run dependency scanners automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides analysis workflows and examples. The user or agent must choose which commands to run.

faq.question: Which ecosystems does it cover?
faq.answer: It includes guidance for npm, Python, Ruby, Java, Go, Rust, PHP, and .NET dependency files.

faq.question: Can it fix vulnerable packages?
faq.answer: It can recommend update plans and example commands, but changes should be reviewed and tested.

faq.question: Does it handle license compliance?
faq.answer: Yes. It outlines license detection, compatibility checks, and reporting for restrictive or unknown licenses.

faq.question: Is private package data sent to external services?
faq.answer: Only if the user chooses network-backed checks. Private repositories should approve this before scanning.

faq.question: Is the security audit risk blocking?
faq.answer: No. The audit found medium operational risk from documented commands and network checks, not malicious code.
